data.title: Brandywine Realty Trust: The Real Problem Hides In The Debt
data.description:
Father Time has been unkind to REITs. We were hoping that the REITs would roll with the punches and some of the tumult of the past five years would subside in 2026. Well, it looks like the tables have all but turned with a new energy crisis reigniting inflation and economic woes popping up left and right.

Over the past few years, the predominant theme of my coverage of REITs has been that not all are created equal. There are important factors that separate the wheat from the chaff. Perhaps the most relevant these days is balance sheet management.

Today, we will revisit Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN), an office REIT that ran into a wall over the past several years. BDN owns, operates, and develops high-quality office assets in a handful of major metros like Austin and Philadelphia. More recently, the company expanded by developing new assets like life science and hotels.

Like many peers, BDN has run into trouble over the past ten years, with shares declining by about 80%.

This article will follow up on my prior coverage of BDN and explain why the challenges are only beginning.

Review of Prior Coverage

Over the past few years, I have covered BDN on a variety of occasions. Much of my coverage of the REIT has been critical, including an article where I said that BDN was charging headfirst into trouble. This article talked about BDN’s hopes to outgrow their troubles. While management was hoping that new assets would produce enough cash flow to maintain the distribution, the company’s cash available for distribution sat well above 100%.

Since publication, shares are down by 35%, and the dividend was slashed by around half.

At the end of 2025, I published another article on BDN. This article dove into the portfolio and explained how the upcoming vacancy from IBM (IBM) created a major cash flow crunch for the REIT. Around 5% of the company’s revenue would disappear in Austin, TX, where vacancy remained around 25%, and BDN was working on a plan to replace the tenant.

IBM (IBM) is BDN’s largest tenant accounting for around 5% of rent. IBM leases around 600,000 square feet in Austin at a massive campus called Uptown ATX. IBM has already decided to exit, according to The Real Deal, leaving BDN to try an reposition the campus for a new tenant.

If we are to look at this situation through the lens of a company that just took a 20% rent reduction in the same market to retain a tenant, this situation gives me goosebumps.

The issue with the IBM lease is replacing the lost rent while spending a minimal amount of money to do so. Looking at the basic economics in the Austin market, including a vacancy rate over 20%, this looks very challenging.

Historically, I have been critical of BDN’s leasing performance. BDN says that they intend to grow their life science business to 25% of pro forma rent.

This comes at a time when they remain unable to lease 3151, their trophy life science asset in Philadelphia. As of January 31, the company was still shopping the asset to office tenants in an effort to fill the space.

The approach has not necessarily made sense to me. Pushing deeper into life science seems inopportune. Just take Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) as an example of how the industry is doing.

Both articles focused on different parts of the BDN puzzle. Initially, we talked about the difficult math behind the company’s cash flow and dividend. Then, we talked about problems in the company’s core markets, including the IBM lease in Austin.

Let’s shift gears and look at a more difficult part of BDN’s problem.

Downgraded…

S&P Global (SPGI) recently published an updated research report on BDN. The company downgraded BDN’s unsecured debt, assigning a BB- credit rating.

We downgraded the company to ‘BB-’ from ‘BB.’ Our outlook remains negative.

The research report went much deeper into BDN’s financials, overviewing some of the more challenged pieces of the business. Included in the S&P report was a discussion of the upcoming Austin maturity, which is expected to further pressure cash flow.

Below is an excerpt from the report.

Our downgrade reflects risk related to BDN’s short-weighted-average debt maturity. We typically view companies with short debt maturity ladders (less than three years for real estate entities) as having greater refinancing risk than peers with longer weighted-average debt maturities. Brandywine’s weighted-average debt maturity fell below three years, and we expect it to remain there at least for the next year. Improvement will depend on how it addresses upcoming maturities, particularly in 2027 when $700 million matures. The company’s near term maturities are modest, including approximately $88.8 million in total loans secured by its not yet stabilized Uptown mixed use project in Austin, Texas in June...

The negative outlook reflects the potential for credit metrics to remain elevated

S&P cited a couple of key drivers behind their decision to downgrade BDN. Operational challenges around leasing and a thin fixed charge coverage ratio of 1.7x, down from 2.1x a year prior, mean that there is little margin for error. Despite multiple dividend cuts and years of portfolio reshaping, BDN is still walking a financial tightrope.

However, the most significant factor by far in S&P’s decision to downgrade BDN is the company’s short debt maturity ladder. Let’s pull open the company’s most recent financials.

Through the end of 2030, BDN has a significant amount of unsecured debt maturing. Next year, there is $700 million, and through the end of 2029, the debt load goes much higher to just short of $2 billion. This presents a significant refinancing risk to BDN, especially as the company’s equity value continues to deteriorate.

In this case, share price matters…a lot. A lower share price might not be the gift you think it is.

We should understand exactly what makes this a risk for BDN.

What Could Go Wrong?

I just published an article on Service Properties Trust (SVC). The article detailed the company’s recent issuance of $500 million in equity to cover an upcoming debt maturity. After several difficult years, SVC approached the debt wall similar to what BDN is facing. With the stock price declining significantly, SVC needed capital to address the maturity. This forced the company to issue several hundred million shares, severely diluting the equity pool, to meet the upcoming payment.

Issuing such a large number of shares creates a math problem. With 166 million shares outstanding as of year-end, the issuance will approximately triple the outstanding share count. Looking forward for SVC, issuing hundreds of millions of new shares more than doubles the existing share count. This makes the possibility of cash flow per share making a rebound much more difficult since cash available for distribution will now be further divided by more shares.

Sometimes a company is forced to face the music. In this case, SVC may have struggled to secure a loan to refinance the maturing debt, a situation reminiscent of what crushed many REITs during the financial crisis.

Let’s shift back towards BDN and ask ourselves if the company could be headed in a similar direction.

Around 90% of the debt on the company’s debt matures in the next four years, most of which is unsecured.

Financials are deteriorating and continue to do so because of the negative impact of past refinancings and leasing woes, including 3151 Market remaining mostly vacant. The company is staring down around 5% of their rental revenue vanishing with a large capital outlay to replace.

All the while, the company paid out just shy of 130% of distributable earnings last year.

That doesn’t give me much comfort that the ship has begun to turn around. With the clock ticking, next year’s debt maturities are creeping up rather quickly.

What to Look for…

So, what exactly should we be looking for to sniff for trouble? Beyond the decline in BDN’s stock price, the future remains unwritten, and BDN could be poised for a comeback. Positive signs could include early successful refinancings of next year’s debt or improvements to the company’s financial metrics, such as the fixed charge coverage ratio and debt to EBITDA.

The most significant risk factors that continue to pressure these areas are interest rates and asset-level performance. With more upcoming vacancies and a rush to lease existing space, BDN is working hard to keep their portfolio at an occupancy rate over 90%. Should the upcoming IBM vacancy present more challenges than expected, it could present compounded pain for BDN.

Without a clear solution to replace the lost cash flow, lenders could begin to scrutinize whether they will refinance the hundreds of millions in upcoming debt. The downgrade to BB- doesn’t bode particularly well. Let’s imagine, under a worst-case scenario, that BDN is forced to refinance the $450 million of unsecured debt by issuing stock.

This would require BDN to double the existing share count based on today’s price and equity market capitalization, implying no price dilution for the issuance. This would likely spell disaster for BDN and shareholders.

It’s not unprecedented either. Recently, Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP) was forced to pull the equity lever, despite their impacted stock price.

Conclusion

BDN has Spaces That Inspire, but not a stock that does. The challenges facing BDN over the past few years have proven impactful to the small but mighty REIT. Today, the company faces significant challenges, including huge development spending, upcoming vacancies, and maturing debt that will challenge their capital markets access. These risk factors have culminated in S&P downgrading BDN’s unsecured debt to BB-.

With risk factors piling up and the market remaining challenging, BDN earns a Strong Sell rating.

data.title: BSTZ: Better Positioned To Capture Growth Of The AI Market
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ), I issued a buy rating due to the attractive outlook at the time. Since then, there have been some shifts within the technology sector, and sentiment has rapidly changed. Investors are a bit concerned about the moat of SaaS companies, which sparked a sell-off of the indices. However, BSTZ has held up a bit better than the rest of the market, so I wanted to revisit the overall value proposition through 2026. Additionally, the fund has released an updated annual report that provides some insights into the fund's earnings.

At the time of my last coverage, BSTZ traded at a discount to NAV of 7.6%. Following the same pullback in the fund, BSTZ now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 9.22%. Referring to the red line in the graph below, we can see that BSTZ trades at the higher end of its five-year price-to-NAV range. Despite this, I believe that the discount window will eventually close as interest rates trend lower and operating spreads improve. BSTZ is also directly aligned to participate in the expansion of the AI market through its exposure to semiconductor companies.

What makes BSTZ unique is the high starting dividend yield of 8.8%. The fund issues those payouts on a monthly basis, and the latest annual report indicates that its portfolio strategy was capable of generating sufficient earnings without an issue. However, there are still some vulnerabilities that investors must be aware of. If the fund is unable to capture net realized gains from its positions, BSTZ may be faced with the scenario of paying out more than it earns. This can lead to NAV erosion and the inability to provide attractive returns.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, BSTZ has total managed assets of $1.67B that are spread across 82 different positions. One of the defining features of BSTZ is the fact that it focuses on finding the next generation of technology companies, rather than focusing on the Mag 7. If management is able to select the right securities, this provides the fund with the potential to locate hidden gems that can lead to massive returns. Expanding on this, BSTZ is able to invest a portion of its assets into private, late-stage companies, which introduces the potential for rapid capital appreciation when things go as planned.

Looking at the portfolio breakdown, we can see that BSTZ is most heavily allocated to semiconductors and semiconductor equipment investments at 29.27% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to tech hardware and software companies, which accounts for 25.05% and 24.79% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up over 79% of total assets. Therefore, the fund is extremely concentrated on semiconductor and technology companies, which can be both a strength and a weakness based on market cycles.

The market has continued to sell off software companies that may be threatened by the cost-efficiency and ease-of-use of AI tools. While BSTZ has been slightly impacted by these shifts, the fund has held up a lot better than the rest of the industry due to the primary allocation to semiconductors. The caveat is that semiconductors are already seeing strong growth in their baseline revenues, which indicates the ability to capitalize on rising demand.

When looking at the holdings of the fund, we can see that BSTZ has exposure to a range of high-quality positions. Furthermore, the fund mixes its allocation between large-cap companies and small- or mid-cap companies. For instance, the largest position in Databricks is a private company that makes up 16.31% of assets. We also get direct exposure to private opportunities like Anthropic and GrubMarket. These private positions are balanced out by public holdings like NVIDIA (NVDA), Lumentum Holdings (LITE), and Tower Semiconductor (TSEM), just to name a few.

An important difference from my last coverage is that BSTZ no longer implements options as part of its strategy. The elimination of options means that BSTZ is fully capable of participating in the growth of its positions now. Option strategies help a fund generate higher income through option premiums, but they cap the upside growth of a fund. This better positions the fund to provide meaningful capital appreciation through bull markets.

Performance Insights & Risks

The fund released an updated annual report for 2025 that provides some insights into the strengths and weaknesses of its earnings. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.26 per share, while net realized gains landed at $4.91 per share. Looking back over prior years, the fund has consistently reported losses in net investment income, though. This can be an indication of the fund's high operating expenses and the limited income that is received from technology companies. It does make sense since technology companies aren't known for providing large dividends to shareholders.

The issue with this structure is that the fund becomes entirely reliant on net realized gains to fuel its NAV growth and support distributions. This makes it difficult to estimate how the fund will perform through challenging market conditions. For instance, a period where the sector faces a large drawdown can be a large issue. For instance, this was the issue in 2022's drawdown, which the fund still struggles to recover from. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $38.82 per share
  • 2022: $19.53 per share
  • 2023: $21.43 per share
  • 2024: $23.14 per share
  • 2025: $24.98 per share

This reliance on net realized gains means that BSTZ has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions to support the distributions. Over a longer holding period, this will likely translate to limited price appreciation, unless we see a rapid rally similar to 2021. For instance, BSTZ's share price has increased by about 11.9% since its inception. However, the total return when including distributions jumps up to 111.3% over the same time frame.

Although BSTZ's total return is attractive, it may not be the most efficient way to get exposure to the sector. For young investors that want to focus on maximizing total return, a more traditional ETF like iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) may be a better choice. The focus on dividends may be actively stealing from the fund's future returns, as we can see in the performance comparison below. For instance, SOXX's total return of 437% crushes BSTZ's return since inception.

Outlook

BSTZ is directly positioned to capture the growth of the AI sector going forward, despite its structural flaws. Although the market is struggling with the sentiment around software, I believe that the market will eventually shift to focus on the benefits for operational efficiency and move into the monetization of AI agents. Through BSTZ's exposure to private entities, the fund is capable of benefitting from the potential of these companies going public at some point.

For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can grow to $3.5T by 2033. This represents an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6%. Since the fund no longer uses options that can cap its upside growth, BSTZ is capable of growing its share price alongside the rest of the market. After reviewing its holdings, I was able to locate some positions that are already growing earnings due to AI:

  • Lumentum Holdings: building out a new manufacturing facility to produce advanced lasers, and Nvidia will be the customer.
  • Anthropic: recently raised its 2026 revenue forecast by 20%, up to $18B.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1625 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.8%. If we assume that BSTZ keeps these distributions the same for the next twelve months, the fund will pay out an estimated annual distribution of $1.95 per share. Referring to the same annual report for 2025, the fund has demonstrated its ability to support distributions with no issues, as long as net realized gains are captured. For instance, the fund experienced total earnings of $4.65 per share, which was strong enough to support multiple years' worth of coverage.

2025's earnings of $4.65 per share / $1.95 per share annual payout = ~2.38 years.

Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that the fund will adjust payouts as a reflection of the market conditions and internal performance. So after the fund realized large losses in 2022, the payouts were reduced. Conversely, BSTZ had a strong 2025, so the fund issued a large supplemental payout to share the excess earnings with shareholders. So while I have no concerns about dividend sustainability, investors should be aware of the potential for variable payouts over time.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from BSTZ may not offer complete tax efficiency. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that the majority of payouts were classified as net realized short-term gains. These distributions are classified as ordinary income and have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in BSTZ is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on BSTZ at this time. The fund still trades at an attractive price-to-NAV valuation, and the removal of its option strategy means that it can fully participate in the upside growth of the market. As the AI market expands, BSTZ is directly positioned to capture the growth of its public and private entities. The exposure to private investments provides investors with the opportunity to get exposure to unique pre-IPO holdings that can turn into massive gains in the future. Furthermore, the fund has demonstrated that it can easily support distributions when market conditions allow. The only risk is BSTZ's reliance on net realized gains to fuel growth can be an issue if market momentum slows. The fund may be faced with the scenario of paying out distributions that it didn't actually earn, which can lead to NAV erosion.

data.title: Hawaiian Electric Industries: Regulatory Relief Cannot Offset The Dilution Overhang
data.description:
On December 20th, I initiated coverage of HE with a BUY, which has so far achieved a 33% return. However, I believe that the prospects of the Iran war change the overall scenario of the stock, causing a significant worsening of the company's capital structure, downgrading the target price from the previous US$ 18.95 to US$ 13.95.

Furthermore, it is still recommended to read the previous article if you want to better understand my view on the company, given that the Guidance, Payments, and Hawaii sections remain the same.

Company Overview:

Hawaiian Electric Industries (HE), founded in 1891, is a holding company based in Honolulu, providing electric services to approximately 95% of the state of Hawaii. The company is currently facing serious difficulties following a large-scale wildfire that left the company with a liability of ~$1.91 billion to be paid by 2029 (cash for 2026 already reserved), in addition to high CAPEX needs for existing grid improvements. The company holds a market cap of 2.53 billion and a value of US$ 14.67 per share. Some of its characteristics are:

  • Beta: 0.57
  • P/E (LTM): US$ 0.76
  • P/E (FWD): US$ 1.01
  • Dividend yield (FWD): None
  • EV/EBITDA: 9.3x
  • 52-Week Range: US$ 9.06-US$ 17.38

It is important to emphasize that my thesis, written in December, was based on two main pillars:

  1. The market overpriced the risk of a bankruptcy.
  2. The company would recover with regulatory help.

And although both points have become true, given the recent macroeconomic events, in my opinion, the recovery will be slower than I originally expected, justifying a downgrade of the company to a HOLD at current prices.

Sector

An important note to have before proceeding with the analysis is that among all sectors, the Utilities Sector is the only one that remains positive in the YTD context. With the S&P 500 showing a drop of -4.65% since the beginning of the year, the Utilities Sector is presenting an appreciation of +6.28%, having a track record of staying positive during all recessions. Part of the appreciation recently presented by HE is a simple consequence of a defensive sector during a difficult period. And, in my opinion, the current political environment will continue helping positively with the sector's multiples for the coming years. Justifying a P/E multiple projection of 16.5x for HE, significantly below the sector's 19.5x due to having no dividends and being in a restructuring phase but still being positively affected by it.

4Q 2025

On February 27, 2026, the company released its results for FY 2025. With an EPS of US$0.71, a significant improvement compared to the negative US$10.42 recorded in 2024. However, when comparing the CORE Earnings of the company, excluding non-recurring items, we obtain a value of US$ 0.86 per share in 2025, a drop of 39.4% compared to the US$ 1.42 in 2024.

The drop presented in 2025 is the result of two main factors, the first being the massive dilution of the shares that occurred in 2024 to pay the first installment of the civil settlement. The second being that the cost inflation for electrical equipment reached 40% just in the last 12 months. 14x higher than the American inflation in the same period. Significantly punishing the company that operates under the ARA remuneration model. The company also continues with a B+ credit rating; although an upgrade from the previous rating, it is still in the junk range, justifying high interest and financial expenses (which should increase for the payment of future installments).

ROE

The company obtained an ROE significantly below expectations, of only 6.7%, where the allowed target is 9.5%. Caused by high financial costs and the inflation of electrical inputs. With this justification, the company filed a Rebasing Docket requesting a revenue increase of US$ 170 million. Such Docket was filed together with Ulupono, a climate protection agency. Both advocate that if the Docket is not approved, the company will not be able to meet the climate transition goals of 100% renewable by 2045. Historically, Dockets commissioned with protection agencies (consumers, climate) guarantee about 90% of the requested amount. Which will serve as the basis for my analysis.

However, even with this increase of US$ 150 million, the company would be able to raise the ROE to 8.5% to 8.9%. In my opinion, this return will stay on the lower limit (lower side) of this range, given the structural flaws of Hawaii combined with the probable increase in defaults generated by the high price of oil because of the war in Iran. While its ROE is depressed at 6.7%, the company generates less internal cash flow, forcing it to rely on external capital to cover its massive obligations. Restoring this ROE back to the 8.5%–8.9% range is essential to recovering.

Equity Issuance

HEI already possesses the necessary cash (restricted cash) to bear the first installment of the payment; however, its liquidity remains pressured to bear the operational costs. With oil at +US$ 100, a higher default is probable. With the operational costs, CapEx needs, and the second installment of the settlement in mind, a B+ credit rating would mean the company would incur extremely punitive interest rates. To deal with the capital need and bypass the resulting high interests, the management reiterated its At-the-Market (ATM) program to raise 250 million.

With a share price of US$ 15, this equals an issuance of 16.6 million new shares, a dilution of approximately 10%. Furthermore, to bear the payment of US$ 479 million in 2027 and bear the rest of the operational capital, HE will probably be forced to enter the convertible debt market to avoid 2-digit interest rates. Assuming a $500 million convertible issuance with a 25% conversion premium (US$ 18.75), this would add another 26.6 million shares, bringing the total to 215.8 million by the end of 2027.

Valuation

Estimating an average CapEx addition according to the guidance of the next 3 years, and consequently a 4% depreciation, an equity portion of the rate base of 30% with an ROE of 8.6%, and an estimated share dilution to 215.8 million, I reach a fair price of US$ 13.95 per share, or a drop of -5% from the current share value of US$ 14.67.

The reason that, despite the drop, I recommend a HOLD instead of a SELL is because I believe for those who can hold on, in 2028 the scenario for the stock will be more positive, with dividends closer to occurring and half of the environmental debts being paid. Besides a massive increase in the rate base financed by the Recovery Bonds.

But while this future does not become more imminent, my recommendation under the current price is HOLD with a target price of US$ 13.95.

Drivers & Risks

A full approval of the Docket justified by Hawaii's aggressive climate goals would bring the company's ROE closer to 8.9–9.0%. This higher ROE is the main driver for the valuation because it directly reduces the need for dilution. Since the company does not pay dividends, all the extra profit generated by a higher ROE is retained as cash. With more internal cash flow, HE would need to issue fewer shares in 2027 to pay its settlement obligations. By keeping the total number of outstanding shares smaller, the EPS denominator is protected, which would already result in a small upside for the company, even in 2027.

On the other hand, the opposite is true. In case there is a lack of political capital for the filing of the Docket, coming from the already high energy costs in Hawaii (double the national average) together with the high costs of passing the fuel to the clients' bills, the positive scenario predicted for 2028 would only occur from 2029 onwards, after the full payment of the environmental debt and the dilutions that would come from it.

Conclusion

Previously, the prices of Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. were unduly compressed by the legal obligations that the company obtained after the 2023 fires. However, I believe that the market has already removed the risk of bankruptcy from the stock prices, leaving only the remaining upside of an accelerated recovery, which, in my opinion, is unlikely.

For these reasons, my final recommendation is a HOLD, with a target price of US$ 13.95.

data.title: SPLV: Recent Outperformance Is Unsustainable
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Low Volatility ETF (SPLV), launched on 05/05/2011 and managed by Invesco Capital Management LLC, offers differentiated exposure to the S&P 500 by targeting the least volatile names in the index. It charges an expense ratio of 0.25% and has $7.2 billion in AUM.

Its methodology differentiates it from its peers in that it lacks sector weight constraints and weights by volatility. But this has resulted in underperformance over the years and is tied to higher sector concentration and interest-rate risk. More importantly, the current market and macroeconomic conditions make this fund more likely to underperform the broad market.

Methodology

SPLV tracks the S&P 500 Low Volatility Index, which starts with the S&P 500 Index (the parent index) and selects the 100 stocks with the lowest volatility. It defines volatility by the standard deviation of each stock's daily price returns over the prior 252 trading sessions.

Instead of market-cap-weighting these 100 stocks, the index also uses volatility as the weighting factor; the lower the volatility, the larger the weight.

Rebalancing occurs on a quarterly basis (each January, April, July, and October) and it becomes effective after the close of the third Friday of February, May, August, and November.

It's noteworthy that the index won't place sector caps, so whatever sectors happen to have the least volatile stocks will constitute most of the portfolio.

Portfolio

As expected, the top weights represent an insignificant portion of the whole portfolio, keeping single-name concentration very low. The top 10 holdings account for only 12.8% of the portfolio and each one of them accounts for around 1.3%.

The sector composition, however, has some noteworthy biases. The first is the long-duration/rate-sensitive exposure, as represented by Tech, Utilities, and Real Estate, which together constitute as much as ~43% of the fund. Stocks in this bucket tend to benefit from falling or stable long-end yields, driven by easing expectations, which are themselves anchored to softer inflation pressure. Basically, exactly the scenario that doesn't characterize the current environment.

However, there is some balance between defensive and cyclical exposure. Consumer Defensive and Health Care stocks account for 18.7% of the portfolio, while Industrials, Consumer Cyclical, Basic Materials, and Energy companies account for 20.1% of the fund. This is important for taking advantage of periods marked by healthy domestic economic momentum, while hedging during slowing growth and elevated recession risk.

Performance

SPLV has tracked its benchmark very closely. On a NAV basis, the ETF has returned 0.27% less than its index over the last year, 0.28% annualized less over the last 5 years, and 0.29% annualized since inception.

Over the long run, SPLV has underperformed some of its most prominent peers. The State Street SPDR SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (LGLV), which has a lower expense ratio at 0.12%, has had the best total-return performance and is significantly better than SPLV's. The iShares MSCI USA Min Vol Factor ETF (USMV) realized a higher total return, too, and charges an expense ratio of 0.15%.

The lower representation of growth stocks, which often exhibit high volatility, implied by the lack of sector constraints, helps explain SPLV's underperformance. In other words, the low-volatility bias of the ETF seems to have been responsible for concentration in sectors that include the least volatile stocks. This factor has excluded high-growth sectors from participating more prominently.

For instance, LGLV doesn't have a hard sector cap, but it applies a constraint by selecting the least volatile stocks in each sector until the aggregated market cap of the selected stocks reaches 30% of the total market cap of the sector. Currently, tech stocks account for 12% of its portfolio (SPLV's tech sector weight is currently 2.8%). Similarly, USMV has the constraint that its sector weights should not deviate by more than 5% from its parent index and its tech exposure is currently 26%.

The silver lining is that SPLV's worst year was significantly less severe in the last 10 years. However, its standard deviation and maximum drawdown don't stand out. As a result, its Sharpe ratio is the lowest, making it also the worst-performing on a risk-adjusted basis.

Relative to broad market exposure, SPLV is far more attractive right now. YTD, it has returned 3% while the S&P 500 is down 4.3%, thanks to multiple fundamental differences.

One of them is the far lower tech exposure of SPLV; 2.8% versus ~33% for the S&P 500. There has not only been a decline in enthusiasm for mega-cap tech stocks (and Mag7 in particular), but also a broad software stock selloff, which has been a significant drag on performance.

Outlook

The main issue, going forward, is that the Mag7/software selloff is probably overdone. First of all, four of the Magnificent 7 are trading below their 200DMA, with the rest being very close. Also, as you can see below, the group has closed below its lower Bollinger band (2.5σ and 50DMA) multiple times lately, reflecting a violent selloff, rather than an orderly rerating.

The same thing happened to the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF, which represents software stocks. The selloff was quick and severe, with the price falling and remaining below the lower Bollinger band for more than a week before entering a consolidation phase and is now well below the 50DMA and 200DMA.

Part of the lost enthusiasm towards mega caps is connected to the increased investments in AI infrastructure by hyperscalers. The market may be demanding more evidence that the payoff is near and meaningful before it keeps bidding up names such as Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG). However, all of those companies refer to supply being outpaced by demand, so the CapEx guidance that got them in trouble in the first quarter of 2026 may be warranted after all.

As for the software stocks, the case against them is even weaker because the disruption by AI, although a reasonable prospect, is unlikely to be an across-the-board phenomenon. The idea is that AI agents can threaten the margins of software businesses, which rely on selling multiple seats per subscription, because they can reduce the number of seats a client needs. The counterargument is that a nearly 30% drawdown is an overreaction when the disruption should be confined to those businesses that differentiate with interface, rather than a competitive database.

All in all, what led to SPLV outperforming lately is not a set of enduring conditions that a careful investor can rely on.

But there's more. As we saw in the Portfolio section, SPLV is significantly exposed to sectors with equities very sensitive to interest rate expectation shifts. The S&P 500 is even more exposed, so this is not an argument for a worse relative positioning. It simply hints at an absolute headwind that, combined with relatively good performance in 2026 so far, amplifies the risk for holding this fund. The Fed maintained the Fed Funds rate at 3.50% to 3.75% in the last meeting and the 30-Day Fed Funds futures prices now imply a 0% probability for a 25 bps cut in the next one, down from 23.3% just a month ago.

Conclusion

SPLV is one of the most prominent options for low-volatility exposure and its strong low-volatility bias differentiates it from other funds. However, the current conditions don't warrant a tactical tilt to this fund right now, as it's unlikely to perform better than vanilla broad-market exposure. For a differentiated exposure to the U.S. equities with a better chance to outperform the market, it's worth considering Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF (RWL), which I recently wrote about here.

What do you think? Please, leave a comment below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Newell Brands: Likely A Value Trap Amid Macro Uncertainties
data.description:
Newell Brands (NWL) currently offers a 8.5% dividend yield, which may be attractive for many income focussed investors, especially amid all the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties. Although the firm has been a reliable payer of dividends for 26 years by now, I believe it is not worth owning the company for these quarterly payments right now. First of all, companies in the consumer discretionary sector may be struggling in the coming quarters, due to low consumer confidence levels. Second, the firm's profitability may also be deteriorating going forward, due to the elevated energy prices. Last, but not least, if energy prices were to remain elevated for longer and demand was to remain soft, the dividend may become unsustainable.

Let's dive deeper into each of these points.

Financials and the macro landscape

NWL's most recent quarterly earnings report showed that net sales declined by 2.7% year-over-year, that the gross profit margin contracted, and that the net losses widened significantly, totalling in $315 million. While the net loss may not be as bad as one would first think, as it was a one-off, non-cash impairment related to indefinite-lived tradenames, the other two are definitely something to be more concerned about.

Let us focus now a bit on the sales part of things. Core sales actually declined by as much as 4.1% compared to the prior year period, but it was partially offset by the favourable exchange rate environment. If we look at the results on a segment basis, we can see that the core sales growth was negative across all of them, with home and commercial solutions, and outdoor and recreation posting the worst numbers. If we break down the sales geographically, we can also see that the core sales growth was negative both in North America and internationally.

Looking forward, the firm expects core sales to keep declining in Q1 and also in full year 2026. Normalised EPS is expected to turn positive, however, for the full year.

To assess how realistic these expectations are, let us look at the macroeconomic environment. The first indicator that I would like to discuss is the consumer confidence. Consumer confidence is a leading economic indicator, which is often used to gauge the potential changes in the spending behaviour of the people in the near term. In the United States, sentiment remains to be poor. Reading are currently hovering just above the 10-year low. Low consumer confidence means that people are not certain about their financial outlook, or the health of the economy overall. As a result, they likely spend less money on discretionary, non-essential, durable goods. This could have a negative impact on the demand for NWL's products. I believe this is fairly reflected in the firm's guidance based on the sales expectations.

When it comes to the cost side of things, operating margins, and cash flows, I am somewhat less optimistic. With the outbreak of the Iran war, energy prices skyrocketed. The price of Brent crude oil is above $110 per barrel, compared to roughly $60 where it was in mid-December 2025. If energy prices were to remain elevated for longer, this could lead to a significant increase in transportation and manufacturing costs. If the firm is not able to pass on these elevated costs to its customers - which I believe is going to be difficult as the demand is already soft - then the margins are likely to contract further, putting downward pressure on the bottomline results.

For these reasons, I do not believe that NWL's business is currently attractive fundamentally. In my view, the macroeconomic headwinds are just too strong.

I would be able to reconsider my bearish view, if there was a material change in the situation in the Middle East. If the Strait of Hormuz was to open up, and energy prices were to moderate, then I would have more faith in the provided EPS guidance. Looking further ahead, I would like to see profitability metrics consistently and sustainable improve, before I could consider investing in NWL.

Dividends

NWL has been a reliable payer of dividends for two and a half decades. Although they had to cut the payments in the recent years, and kept the dividend at the same level since then, they at least kept paying. The current quarterly dividend is $0.07, equivalent to an annual yield of 8.5%. This may look appealing at first, but I believe it may not sustainable, if the macroeconomic environment does not improve soon.

Why am I so pessimistic about the dividend? On one hand it is about sustainability of the payments, on the other hand it is about the consistency of the earnings and the cash flow.

If we look at the cash from operation, CAPEX and dividend figures from the most recent quarters, we can see that the free cash flow was able to cover the dividends 7 out of 11 times, but 4 times it was not. In general, I like dividend paying companies, which can consistently generate more free cash flow than their dividend payments.

If we look at the current dividend safety grade assigned to the stock on Seeking Alpha, we can see that it has a relatively poor, D- grade. In the following table, all of NWL's metrics appear to be poorer than those of the respective sector median.

If the earnings were to deteriorate further, due to the macroeconomic headwinds, the dividend safety metrics are likely to get even worse - in my opinion-, which could lead to further dividend cuts in the future.

Valuation

One may say that the fundamentals may not be the best, but at least the valuation looks appealing. Well, it may look appealing at first, as the company is selling at a significant discount compared to its own historic metrics, as well as compared to the consumer discretionary sector median.

If we narrow down the peer group to a handful of firms that have a business profile similar to that of NWL, we can still see that NWL appears cheap.

In my view, the attractive valuation at this point, without the strong fundamentals, or macro tailwinds, is just an indication of a value trap. The recent insider activity is also not helping. There was significant insider selling in the past months. If the valuation was truly attractive, insiders would likely be picking up the stock at the current price level. But they are selling.

All in all, I do not find NWL attractive from a valuation point of view.

Conclusions

All in all, I believe that the current fundamentals justify a sell rating. Declining sales, poor outlook, significant macroeconomic headwinds, driven by the low consumer sentiment levels, as well as the elevated oil prices, are all putting downward pressure on the demand and on the profitability metrics.

While the valuation may look appealing, the stock is cheap for a reason, and I do not believe that the firm offers a significant upside potential from the current levels. Especially, when we take into account that the dividend may be in danger.

data.title: MSC Industrial Direct's Dip After Earnings Isn't A Reason To Buy
data.description:
Frankly, I find myself a bit surprised today. After reporting financial results for the second quarter of its 2026 fiscal year, MSC Industrial Direct (MSM) saw its share price drop around 0.8%. I am not surprised that it declined during a day when the market was up nicely. In fact, I am surprised that the stock didn't fall further. Revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share, all fell short of what analysts were hoping to see. However, the market was likely optimistic about the fact that, year over year, the company is doing quite well. Shares are not what I would consider to be expensive at this time, but they aren't cheap enough to fall into the undervalued category as I typically describe it. That's why I believe that calling it a "Hold" makes the most sense here.

That is what I had the company rated when I last wrote about it in April of last year. Whenever I call a company a "Hold," I am making the claim that the stock should perform similar to the broader market for the foreseeable future. In the time that has passed since then, MSC Industrial Direct has outperformed a bit, rising 22.1% while the S&P 500 is up 17.2%. But because of the strong fundamental performance that the business has exhibited, I can understand some degree of outperformance here. I don't think, however, that the company is worthy of change in my overall rating based on its valuation.

Disappointment across the board

Shares of MSC Industrial Direct ticked slightly lower on April 1st after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. This follows management reporting revenue and profitability that fell short of expectations. However, you should not conflate this with the overall health of the business. In fact, the company, which operates as a leading North American distributor of metalworking, maintenance, repair, and operations, not to mention production fastener and hardware products and services, is doing quite well. For those not familiar with the scale of the enterprise, it operates an entire network of assets that includes 37 warehouses, 5 manufacturing locations, 9 regional inventory centers, and other assets, all dedicated to selling the 2.5 million SKUs that the firm makes available to customers through its e-commerce channels, catalogs, brochures, and more.

Revenue for the quarter ended up being $917.8 million. That translated to a rise of 2.9% compared to the $891.7 million reported a year earlier. According to management, this increase in revenue was the result of $58.8 million of increased prices that the firm charged its customers, as well as a $2.9 million benefit associated with foreign currency fluctuations. Revenue would have been higher had it not been for the fact that volumes pushed sales lower to the tune of $35.6 million. Management claimed that this drop in revenue was driven in part by weather-related conditions and the partial government shutdown, the latter of which pushed public sales down by 1% year over year. Interestingly, management made the case that instead of seeing demand slow down, they are actually seeing demand increase. That seems to be at odds with broader economic conditions, and I have to wonder whether this is just because of a better position that the company has put itself in from a customer relationship and competitive standpoint.

The revenue reported by management happened to be $13.7 million below what analysts were hoping to see. But that wasn't the only area of weakness on a relative basis. Year over year, earnings per share grew from $0.70 to $0.76. But that ended up still being $0.07 lower than what analysts were hoping for. Despite this, it still translated to a rise in net profits from $39.3 million to $42.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the company fell short of expectations also. Adjusted earnings per share rose from $0.72 to $0.82. However, that still ended up being $0.02 below what had been guessed by analysts. Regardless, that still meant a rise in adjusted net income from $40.4 million to $45.8 million. The first chart in this article shows certain cash flow metrics as well. These all improved year over year.

This seems to be part of a broader trend for the company. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue, profits, and two of the three cash flow metrics that I calculated for the business all improved year over year. Adjusted net income increased also. According to management, higher prices added $97.8 million to the top line, while foreign currency contributed another $3.9 million. However, sales volumes are still down, having dropped $38.4 million year over year. This overall decline in volume does suggest to me that while management said that demand is picking up, it is actually quite weak. This does conform with my own view that the economy seems to be heading toward a recession.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety, we don't have a great deal of information to go from to guess what the picture might look like. Management did say that the firm should see some growth in the third quarter. This is even though geopolitical issues, such as the war against Iran and rising fuel costs, have created some uncertainty for the company. There also seem to be risks when it comes to certain cost inputs, such as carbide, which has seen a surge in pricing. Although management has been taking steps to ensure ample supply, carbide itself has been a bit of an issue that has created some supply constraints for the business. Beyond this, however, we are left a little bit in the dark.

The easiest way to value the company would be to project out results based on what we have seen already for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year. Assuming that this trend continues, adjusted net profits should grow from $210 million last year to $239.6 million this year. Adjusted operating cash flow should expand from $329.4 million to $361.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business should increase from $404.4 million to $440.9 million.

If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. On an absolute basis, I would definitely say that the business is not expensive. But it's not in the cheap category either. It sits perhaps near the lower end of the fair value range. Relative to other similar companies, on the other hand, it is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In that same chart, you can see precisely what I mean here. On a price-to-earnings basis, it is cheaper than any of these five businesses. And when it comes to the other two approaches to valuing the company, just two of the five enterprises ended up being cheaper than it is.

Since I prioritize absolute valuation over relative valuation, I naturally would gravitate toward the more conservative approach to valuing the company. But this does not mean that I am anything other than optimistic about it from an operational standpoint in the long haul. Management has a long history of growing the business. For instance, from 2022 through 2025, the firm deployed about $1.5 billion in capital. 23% of this was growth-oriented capital expenditures. Another 7% involved mergers and acquisitions activities. Having said that, I would be derelict in my duties if I did not acknowledge that most of the company's efforts have gone toward returning cash to shareholders. 47% of what the company deployed during this time went to dividends. Another 23% was allocated towards share buybacks.

That trend has continued throughout this year. In the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, the company paid out $97.2 million in dividends to shareholders. It also brought back 160,000 shares of itself in exchange for $13.7 million. I suspect that, throughout the rest of this year, the company will continue to deploy capital in this manner. Given how the stock is priced, my strong preference would be to focus on growth. But if a significant ramping up in growth is not in the cards, then I would prefer some share buybacks over higher dividends. In my view, dividends only make sense when there are literally no other sources of capital that are productive and when shares are very expensive.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I must say that I remain optimistic about MSC Industrial Direct from an operational standpoint. But unfortunately, I cannot in good faith turn bullish on it. That has nothing to do with the fact that management fell short of expectations that analysts had for it. Rather, it stems from the fact that the stock looks more fairly valued than undervalued at this time. Because of this, I will maintain my "Hold" rating on the stock.

data.title: OpenAI Won't Save ARKK
data.description:
As SpaceX (SPACE), Anthropic (ANTHRO), and OpenAI (OPENAI) seemingly race to go public, a trend of ETFs and other funds trying to get exposure to these private ventures pre-IPO has emerged.

I've covered one of these recently, the XOVR ETF, and found that position liquidity was a problem. SpaceX exposure would fluctuate from <2% to >10% due to new AUM not being able to buy into private vehicles fast enough to keep up but being able to deploy into public stocks easily. So while the fund itself still has an interesting premise, its execution isn't what I had envisioned, and I downgraded it to hold. That hasn't stopped other funds from trying a similar tactic of including private stock.

Enter Cathie Wood's ARK Invest and their flagship ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), which I last covered in an article titled “Trading into the Wind,” which has been pining to add private exposure to its holdings since the fairly successful launch of the ARK Venture Fund (ARKVX).

Now that these major private companies are nearing their expected IPOs, ARKK is jumping in with a new addition: OpenAI. But I don't believe it adds to the fund; in fact, I believe it could be a drag for ARKK investors, especially since it is being given tremendous weight in the fund. It further solidifies my Hold rating; ARKK will not outperform the market even with this change.

ARKK Overview

Briefly, here are the statistics for ARKK that investors should be aware of:

Inception: October 2014
Price: $68.50
Div Freq: Annual
Div Rate: ~0.80%
AUM: $5.97B
ER: 0.75%
Holdings: 45

For those new to ARKK, I wrote the educational piece on ARKK for Seeking Alpha, where I covered its history and the kinds of risks that investors should know when investing in this fund.

Here's a relevant excerpt from that article that gives some context on ARKK for our purposes today:

[ARKK] invests in a wide swath of ideas, aiming to hold the "best of the best" stocks in each of its categories. It is willing to hold on to some big losers because some of its big winners often outpace them, or at least that's the idea. To win, one necessarily has to sometimes lose, as the mindset of this kind of investing approach goes.

Naturally, that makes the innovation bet a speculative venture, and is largely suitable for aggressive investors only...

ARK Invest offers the following fund description on their website that provides their definition of innovation, as every fund that offers this kind of speculative approach has its own definitions for what is and isn't "innovation."

"ARKK defines "disruptive innovation" as the introduction of a technologically enabled new product or service that potentially changes the way the world works.

Companies within ARKK include those that rely on or benefit from the development of new products or services, technological improvements, and advancements in scientific research relating to the areas of:

  • Intelligent Devices
  • Autonomous Mobility
  • Precision Therapies
  • Neural Networks
  • Next Gen Cloud
  • Digital Wallets
  • Digital Assets
  • Smart Contracts
  • Multiomic Technologies."

ChatGPT, Meet ARKK

In the ARK strategy, which I've long called “the shotgun approach,” what is currently hot is what the next move is. So it wasn't long before private assets started to make their way into ARKK. Given how hot they've been in the last year, and with the upcoming big three AI firms (SpaceX owns xAI now) debuting this year, liquidity may be less of an issue.

It came as little surprise to me when I saw the news of ARK announcing that they will include a ~3% allocation to OpenAI in their flagship ARKK as well as their fintech (ARKF) and internet (ARKW) funds.

That announcement was on March 31st, and as of April 1st, the ARKK holdings now reflect a ~3% position in OpenAI—specifically “PPOPENAIC”—which I'm understanding to mean “private placement OpenAI series C.”

The CUSIP makes it clear that this is the Series C fundraising round, which was conducted earlier that day.

It's been clear in my past analyses of ARKK that I am not a fan of their premise, stock selection criteria, or trading behavior. But I think that the inclusion of OpenAI makes ARKK worse off than it was before.

Admittedly, OpenAI does fit the general premise of most of the stocks in the portfolio. It has several things in common with their largest holdings:

  1. The company's focus is on the bleeding edge of a hot technology.
  2. They have enormous growth potential.
  3. They trade at extreme valuation metrics; the fund's average PE is ~50.
  4. They are highly unprofitable (largely the cause of #3).

But I don't believe that frontier labs, the term for pure-play AI model providers like OpenAI, xAI, and Anthropic, will be the real winners of the AI rush. They produce a commodity that people have little loyalty or fealty toward, instead preferring the cheapest and best-performing model. So a bet on OpenAI is a bet that ChatGPT will continue to dominate market share and be the eventual winner of the AI model competitions.

I argue that OpenAI is not only overvalued in its current form but worth more dead than alive.

The Problem With OpenAI

Participating in the Series C means that ARKK just bought at the highest valuation OpenAI has ever had, now $852B after this funding round, which was also participated in by SoftBank (SFTBY), Nvidia (NVDA), and Amazon (AMZN).

Here's some context on that figure:

But unlike those firms on the right, whose valuations still don't add up to OpenAI's, they are profitable. OpenAI isn't, and not only that, it's currently on track to burn $218B over the next three years. That's more than 10x what Uber (UBER) burned in its path toward profitability.

I understand that sometimes you have to spend money to make money, but I tend to apply that to CapEx purchases. OpenAI is mostly spending this on OpEx, just burning money to run ChatGPT and the other AI models.

We know that even their highest-tier paid option is unprofitable, and their Sora video generation app was so deeply unprofitable that they had to pull the plug on it despite a $1B investment from Disney (DIS), which died with the app. There are now reports that “adult mode” is on pause as well.

It could be a rejiggering of business interests toward intelligence, which is the claim from OpenAI (although I am suspicious of an artificial intelligence company that frames its older business decisions as a shift away from intelligence), or they could be burning cash even faster than expected.

I expect these issues to get worse, especially as ChatGPT loses its scaling benefits. Its market share is shrinking as it loses users to other models. Most recently, Anthropic's Claude has gained significant popularity due to its ongoing spat with the government.

Among enterprise customers, whom I expect to be the largest source of real revenue for these frontier labs, was losing market share from well before then. The paradigm shift started in January, when Anthropic overtook OpenAI in first-timer acquisitions.

To be clear, OpenAI is still the leading AI model provider among businesses. But its early lead from the media attention it received back when it first launched publicly is fading, and the effects of leveraging money now to scale later are looking harder to achieve than they used to.

Anthropic, Google (GOOGL) with its Gemini model, and xAI's Grok model are all on the rise, while OpenAI's market share stagnates.

I am not bullish on any frontier labs and believe that the gains in AI will go to the existing hyperscaler cloud providers while they lease models from these labs and leverage their ownership of the data centers to keep margins high and cash flowing despite their own internal burn spent on LLM tokens.

Ultimately, I expect the large hyperscalers to buy up these model companies once they can no longer sustain the massive cash burns, and profitability stays ever far away. OpenAI is likely worth more dead than alive, and so I'll be avoiding exposure to it in the meantime.

Conclusion

I am maintaining my Hold rating on ARKK and believe that it will not outperform the market; I suspect it may even see negative returns as the large allocation to OpenAI will drag on its returns moving forward.

The inclusion of OpenAI into the ARK funds does not bode well for ARKK's future returns, as it is a firm that looks to be overvalued, unprofitable, and losing the only thing that makes it a viable business: total control of market share. Warning signs are already showing up, with products launched and invested in by serious partners being canceled and a cash burn plan that totals over $200B.

ARKK may be trying to stir up investor interest, and it seems to have worked, as they got headlines about the investment. But investors should be warned that this does not seem to be a prudent investment or one that will pay off anytime soon for ARKK or its investors.

This inclusion is not a complete poison pill for investors who were already bullish on the fund, but it should be a consideration. Even if OpenAI isn't in line, it's likely that more unprofitable model providers will be added to the fund in the future. I would expect Anthropic, which is already held by the AGIX ETF, among others, to be added soon.

An even heavier weighting to frontier model providers would cause me to re-evaluate my position to a sell, most likely.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Urban Outfitters: Nuuly Offers An Overlooked Growth Engine, Backed By A Debt-Free Balance Sheet
data.description:
Introduction

The first time I covered Urban Outfitters (URBN), I called them “A Debt-Free Retailer With Growing Brands And Improving Fundamentals,” highlighting their excellent financials and significant potential to expand in the long-term while navigating near-term macro pressure quite well.

After an overall solid year where the company continued their expansion despite macro headwinds, URBN remains a Buy, being backed by very solid fundamentals, with zero debt and strong brands that can continue their long-term expansion thanks especially to Nuuly, while CAPEX is expected to ramp up even more now thanks to significant investments.

Expansion Continues, Foundation Still Spotless

Despite record holiday sales, URBN missed the market’s EPS expectations while beating on revenue, with significant growth in nearly everything, posting strong growth in most segments of the business while their Nuuly subscription segment grew at a very strong 43% during the quarter, with gross profit improving to a record $599 million during Q4 despite macro weakness and tariff pressure.

URBN’s free cash flow reached a solid $315 million in FY26 (almost identical to the $320 million seen in FY25) despite a significant increase in CAPEX, which is not bad for a ~$5.47 billion market cap given their aggressive expansion, tariff impacts, and overall weak macro environment, potentially being significantly better in case of a better environment.

For FY27, URBN expects to grow their number of stores to 836 (opening 57, closing 14), while CAPEX is expected to jump a lot, planning to spend approximately $385 million, with ~40% going into retail store expansion and support, ~40% into their investments in logistics, and ~20% into technology investments and home office expansion.

Note that their guidance is before the latest tariff announcements, expecting high single-digit growth in total company sales, with mid-single-digit retail segment comps and still mid-double-digit revenue growth at Nuuly even before the tariff reversal.

Financially, based on URBN’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with virtually no debt and current assets that cover their current liabilities well, as well as $369 million in cash and cash equivalents and $327 million in marketable securities. This is ultimately an advantage of URBN, as their lack of debt combined with the fact that they rely on leasing properties can add significant potential to expand while also being more flexible in terms of closing underperforming operations.

URBN's buybacks also nearly tripled last year, completing $154 million worth of buybacks, representing a yield of roughly 2.8% based on current prices, although that’s a very manageable payout ratio of ~49%.

Macro Risks Rising

To start on a positive note, we saw the Supreme Court’s positive tariffs decision, which helped clear some headwinds and uncertainty surrounding the company’s margins, and the COO highlighted a potential upside opportunity of 25 basis points in gross profit margins if the current tariff environment remains, also mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Now on to the current year. The recently announced Supreme Court ruling and subsequent Section 122 announcement certainly change things. These recent events were not contemplated in our original plans for fiscal year 2027, which Melanie is going to talk about in a few minutes. If the Section 122 tariffs stay in place for the year or expire in July, we do believe there could be an incremental IMU benefit to our current plans. There are certainly a lot of external discussions about what will or won't happen over the coming months. So we are cautious about planning for change until there is more clarity. In the meantime, our teams continue to work diligently on all our tariff mitigation efforts.

On top of that, the ongoing Iran conflict can have detrimental effects on the company’s near-term prospects (at least), posing a risk for the demand given the effects on discretionary budgets - as pump prices jumped significantly from day one - while central banks warned about the potential inflationary pressure that can lead to higher interest rates, even though we see the market’s optimism in a potential resolution rise recently.

Again, even though Urban Outfitters is in an arguably strong position and shouldn’t be impacted by higher debt costs that much, we’re still talking about something that can have effects on their clients, partners, and especially the market’s valuation, with worries about this inflation and the Fed’s potential response already putting pressure on their stock price, as treasury yields could also become even more attractive, “forcing” the market to adjust these valuations to a risk-adjusted level given how the yield on treasuries skyrocketed in March.

Still, this company can also benefit from a potential long-term recovery while their Nuuly subscription service continues to grow aggressively, and I’ll mention again that it can offer a very significant advantage that other peers don’t seem to work on.

Ultimately, this can generate very valuable data about their consumers alongside broader trends, generating data about brand power, preferences, design issues, etc. that should allow them to do something similar to what retailers are doing now in the food industry - tailoring their offering to fill in the missing gaps in demand through data analytics, building a potential powerhouse.

Risks Reiterated

Besides trends and other fashion-related industry risks, URBN is always going to depend on the consumer, and given the ongoing macro uncertainty, there’s definitely the risk of seeing an even weaker environment, at least in the foreseeable future, potentially hurting the company’s expansion plans and growth expectations.

Meanwhile, competitive pressure remains very significant, and investors shouldn’t forget that we’re talking about a company that’s still trading at just a fraction of what the industry’s titans trade at - and that’s just the legal side of the competition.

Valuation

For URBN's DCF to Equity, with zero net borrowings given their spotless balance sheet, assuming $385 million in CAPEX, I'll assume they can deliver a FCF of roughly $240 million in 2026 (i.e. their FY’27), which adding back $125 million from 2027 (FY’28) - meaning the CAPEX would fall back to the $260 million seen last year, which is relatively elevated - on top of a 10% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their significant investments, buybacks boosting the per share amounts, and potential for macro improvements.

I’ll assume this is followed by a 7.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 10% to take into account the competitive pressure and demand a good margin of safety, with 91.35 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $6.92 billion, for an intrinsic value of $75.77, which is not far from the levels seen even a few months ago and above the current $62.67 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

URBN remains a Buy, backed by a spotless balance sheet and very significant investments planned for this year, growing well despite the ongoing macro headwinds and standing to benefit in the long-term.

Competition and the economic uncertainty remain significant risks, but the current valuation offers a solid margin of safety, implying quite conservative assumptions already.

data.title: Starbucks: The Financial Brew Sours
data.description:
Starbucks Corp. (SBUX), the world's largest coffee-shop chain, showed signs of a top-line recovery that fueled a February rally, but the fundamental picture remains bleak. A soft rebound in revenue has come at the expense of profit margins, offering little justification for the stock’s rich valuation.

Considering both fundamental and macro headwinds, I initiate coverage with a sell rating and a price target of $42.40, which implies a potential downside of about 50%. With skepticism of a sustained recovery, I apply a 20x forward earnings multiple versus ~39x currently traded.

Margin squeeze

The company eked out 4% comparable sales growth in the latest quarter, including a 3% transaction increase and a 1% average ticket gain. CEO Brian Niccol, who previously steered the successful turnaround of restaurant chain Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), has implemented the “Back to Starbucks” strategy to improve service and cut wait times. However, rising costs are making this strategy less sustainable.

Gross margin shrank to 21.1% in 1Q26, which ended December 2025, from 24.5% a year ago, while operating margin similarly compressed by 290 bps to 9%. Bloated labor costs, driven by more baristas and their training, and higher tech spend to streamline operations have put a squeeze on profitability. As a result, adjusted EPS dropped 19% YoY to 56 cents in the quarter. A sharp decline in GAAP-based earnings was largely attributable to the reclassification of SBUX’s China assets following a stake sale, which led to a surge in recorded tax liabilities.

While the company guides for a 3%-plus increase in 2026 comparable revenue, along with a slight improvement in operating margin, I doubt both goals are achievable at the same time. They can’t have the cake and eat it. For FY26, which ends September 2026, I expect adjusted EPS to stay flat at $2.12, below the low end of SBUX’s guidance range ($2.15-$2.40).

Macro headwinds

Externally, US consumer sentiment has soured into 2026, as high oil prices and AI-driven job losses weigh on household finance. Signs of weakness have emerged across the consumer discretionary spectrum, from fashion retailing to casual dinning, to big-ticket durable goods.

Starbucks sits in a particularly exposed position. A $7 cold brew with oat milk and foam is among the skippable items when purse strings are tightened. Brewing coffee at home is a straightforward and cheap substitute.

Meanwhile, the competitive landscape has shifted against SBUX both in the US and internationally. In America, Dutch Bros (BROS) has been expanding its drive-through model with aggressive pricing and a loyal young customer base. Independent local shops are also luring people with their customization and a sense of community.

In Asia, the picture is arguably worse. Luckin Coffee (LKNCY) has become China's largest coffee chain by store count and is still growing its revenue at double digits. Luckin's aggressive pricing, digital-first model, and integration with China's food delivery platforms have eroded SBUX’s position in what was once its most exciting growth market.

There are other headaches besides rising competition. Hybrid and remote work arrangements have reduced the daily commute ritual that once drove SBUX’s morning traffic. And the “third place” function of coffee shops - a social anchor between home and the office - is on the wane.

In addition, the rise of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, which reduce sugar craving, poses a threat to the company’s high-margin products. At risk are syrup-loaded Frappuccinos, whipped cream toppings, cold foam, and pastries — the add-ons that drive ticket size and margin.

Valuation

Let’s examine how Starbucks stacks up against one of its aggressive rivals. Luckin’s revenue is forecast to rise ~20% this year, following a 43% expansion in 2025, compared with SBUX’s low-single-digit growth. On a key measure of capital efficiency, Luckin’s return on invested capital has stayed in the high-teens region for three years, while SBUX’s ratio has fallen below 7%, near its weighted average cost of capital. An ROIC below cost of capital would suggest a company generates no economic value for investors. Despite higher growth and efficiency, Luckin is trading at around 16x estimated 2026 earnings, compared with ~39x for SBUX.

There’s no doubt that Starbucks remains a strong global brand with a loyal customer base and unmatched footprints across more than 80 countries and regions. I therefore apply a 20x forward earnings multiple, acknowledging the company's strengths while accounting for its weak growth, margin pressure, and structural headwinds. Combining this ratio with my $2.12 estimate for 2026 EPS yields a target price of $42.40, implying a roughly 50% downside.

Key risks

Brian Niccol has a formidable track record of rebuilding Chipotle and transforming it into one of the highest-performing restaurant chains. If his "Back to Starbucks" strategy proves more successful than I expect, through stimulating transaction growth while absorbing costs, the current SBUX valuation will become more defensible.

I could also be wrong about the US consumption outlook. If the job market improves and the oil crisis quickly recedes, Starbucks will find it easier to lure customers back while holding or raising prices.

Conclusion

Starbucks is caught between a rock and a hard place. While the "Back to Starbucks" strategy shows signs of reviving sales, an accompanying margin squeeze raises doubts about its sustainability. Macro headwinds, rising competition and the potential threat of weight-loss drugs all compound the challenge. The company’s valuation looks overstretched under these circumstances, and I apply a much lower earnings ratio to the stock, along with a sell rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Expect SCHD's Stellar Performance To Eventually Give Way To Inferior Returns
data.description:
In early January of this year, I made a rather bold claim that buying Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) would be an awful way to start 2026. To be honest with you, that has not aged well. Or at least, it hasn't aged well up to this point. From the time that article was published until the close of day on March 28th, the S&P 500 (SP500) was down 6.9%. By comparison, the total return of SCHD was up 10.7%. In the short run, this outperformance has been decisive. However, it does echo a previous article that I had written about the ETF last year.

In April of 2025, I warned that SCHD would continue to be a bitter disappointment to investors in all scenarios other than the world falling apart. Fast forward to this year, and that seems to be happening. In the U.S., economic conditions are deteriorating thanks to tariffs and high interest rates. Consumer confidence is near all-time lows. Jobs data is looking worse and worse month after month. And now we have this war with Iran that is creating geopolitical uncertainty and rising oil prices that will further hurt the U.S. This helps to explain why SCHD, which is bolstered by its distributions and the fact that it's overall slate of assets are relatively low risk, outperformed the market leading up to this point.

In the near term, this outperformance is likely to continue. I do think that economic conditions are worsening. And as I argued back in August of last year, the U.S. does seem to be nearing a recession. If economic conditions deteriorate, we could see SCHD deliver quite nicely. However, my focus when it comes to investing is not on the short run. Rather, it is the long term outlook. And when you look at the picture through that lens, SCHD still certainly deserves a Sell rating.

Checking in on SCHD

At first glance, SCHD might seem to be an attractive opportunity for investors. The current yield that it offers right now stands at 3.45%. That's not fantastic, but it's not nothing either. And with interest rates declining, companies and ETFs that previously offered lower yields are suddenly becoming more attractive than they were when interest rates were higher. One thing that certainly helps here is that a lot of the yield that it generates comes from the energy sector. In the chart above, you can see the breakdown of industries that it is exposed to. 19.88% of its assets are tied up in energy plays. And with oil prices (CL1:COM) rising through the roof right now, it stands to reason that this part of its portfolio would be performing exceptionally well.

For context, its largest holding is none other than Chevron Corporation (CVX). It accounts for 4.70% of its assets. Year to date, that stock is up 39.5% because of the surge in oil prices. That's not even the best performer. Its second largest holding is ConocoPhillips (COP), which is even more exposed to changes in oil prices. Shares of that energy play are up 42.6% year to date. If this situation with Iran continues to escalate, further upside for these names and others in its portfolio is probable.

Of course, the return of the ETF is based on returns across all of its assets on a weighted basis. That's why the stock is not up more than it currently is. Consumer staples, for instance, account for about 18.50% of its portfolio. Healthcare amounts to 16.20%. And industrial stocks total 12.10%. We also have meaningful exposure when it comes to financials, consumer discretionary companies, and information technology.

Even though SCHD has outperformed the S&P 500, as well as other major market indices, since I last wrote about it, that does not mean that it has outperform them over the long haul. In fact, over most windows of time, it has indeed been horribly disappointing. In the chart above, you can see that outperformance really began sometime around July of 2025. That's around the time that market volatility started creating issues for market participants. But prior to that point, SCHD has been a loser compared to the S&P 500, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, and the NASDAQ. The most telling example is from the time that I originally rated it a "sell" back in September of 2023. From that time through the time of this writing, the ETF is up 37.4%. The S&P 500, even with recent underperformance, is down 48.1%. By this point, SCHD is still actually outperforming the Dow Jones Industrial Average, if only slightly. But it is drastically trailing the NASDAQ.

What this shows is that, as I said back in April of last year, during times of extreme volatility, you can expect a short-term outperformance. But in the long run, it is structurally inadequate if your objective is long term upside. In fact, I would even argue that it is inferior to a well-structured high dividend portfolio. Back in September of 2023, I decided to put this to the test. I picked out five companies, which would be AT&T (T), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), Dominion Energy (D), Walgreens Boots Alliance, and Citizens Financial Group (CFG). These were chosen based on a few metrics. First off, I wanted them to be very large corporations that were financially stable. Second, I wanted the portfolio to be relatively diverse. Third, I wanted the yields that these opportunities offered to be high enough to be able to perform at or above the yield that SCHD offers, and finally, I wanted to make sure that none of these holdings were owned by SCHD so I could not be accused of cherry picking the best prospects from the ETF while discarding those that would be most likely to underperform.

As some of you likely know, Walgreens Boots Alliance underperformed significantly. The company was eventually taken private, and I subsequently substituted in Sanofi (SNY) in its place. What is interesting here, however, is that even with that laggard, the portfolio that I constructed has continued to outperform pretty much everything else over the long haul. As the table above illustrates, it too has underperformed SCHD in the near term. But since I originally chose those stocks, it is up 64.7%. That beats the 37.4% that SCHD has captured, and even outperforms the NASDAQ. I do not believe that excluding the true loser of the group is the most appropriate way to check out its total performance. But out of curiosity, I could not resist. Had it not been for the horrible play that was Walgreens Boots Alliance, the portfolio that I constructed would still be up 91.2%, or nearly triple what SCHD has captured while coming in around double with the S&P 500 achieved. Even since my last article in January of this year, the portfolio that I constructed has outperformed the broader market. This shows that a well constructed portfolio with even modest diversification is superior in some instances to the market and is, over the long haul, superior to SCHD.

It is worth noting also that the yield offered up is above what SCHD provides. I know that is a big reason for investors buying into the ETF. As I mentioned earlier, it offers a yield right now of 3.45%. But as the chart above illustrates, the portfolio that I constructed has a yield of 3.97% as of this writing. That's not a big disparity, and it certainly is not as big as it was in the past. However, it is hefty enough to be attractive to yield chasing investors.

Takeaway

If you believe that the short term is going to be painful for the market, which I actually do, I can understand why, on a short-term basis, you would actually gravitate toward SCHD. It has clearly proven itself useful in that regard as I predicted that it could back in April of last year. However, if you are also a long-term investor like I am, you will separate out what could be a short term impact of the ETF from the long term reality that it faces. At the end of the day, I prefer to focus on the long haul since that is what matters most. So even though I acknowledge that short term outperformance could continue, I believe that the long-term orientation necessitates that I reaffirm SCHD as a Sell.
